title: May & might â€” possibility
description: Use may and might to talk about things that are possible but not certain in the future.

content.title: May and might â€” expressing possibility
content.body:
We use MAY and MIGHT to talk about things that are POSSIBLE â€” not certain.

MAY = slightly more certain (about 50% chance)
MIGHT = slightly less certain (about 30-40% chance)
In everyday speech, they are often interchangeable.

Examples:
â€¢ I may go to the party â€” I haven't decided yet.
â€¢ It might rain this evening â€” carry an umbrella.
â€¢ She might get the job â€” she interviewed well.
â€¢ We may not make it on time â€” the traffic is bad.

Negative:
â€¢ I may not come tomorrow.
â€¢ He might not know the answer.
content.grammar_note:
may/might + BASE VERB (no 'to', no -ing)
Negative: may not / might not (mayn't is rare; mightn't is informal)
No 's' for he/she/it â€” may/might never change form

content.title: May/might vs will vs going to
content.body:
Compare these levels of certainty:

100% certain:
'I will be there at 6.' (definite)

High certainty (decided plan):
'I'm going to be there at 6.' (already decided)

Possible (not certain):
'I may/might be there at 6.' (not sure yet)

Key tip: When you are NOT SURE if something will happen, use MAY or MIGHT â€” not WILL.

Wrong: 'I will maybe go.' âœ—
Right: 'I might go.' âœ“
Right: 'I may go.' âœ“
content.grammar_note:
will = certain prediction / decided
going to = decided plan / evidence
may/might = possible, not certain

vocabulary.example: I'm uncertain about my weekend plans.

vocabulary.meaning: a chance that something might happen
vocabulary.example: There's a possibility of rain tonight.
She hesitated before accepting the offer.

vocabulary.meaning: to think about something before deciding
vocabulary.example: I'm considering whether to apply or not.

vocabulary.meaning: to be decided by something else

vocabulary.example: It's unlikely to snow in Chennai!

sentences: I might visit my parents this weekend â€” I haven't decided yet.
She may not come to the meeting â€” she's unwell.
It might get cold tonight â€” take a jacket.
We may have to reschedule â€” the venue isn't confirmed.
He might be the right person for the job.

content.question: Priya isn't sure if she can attend the event on Saturday. What does she say?
options: I might attend on Saturday â€” it depends on my family plans.
content.correct: I might attend on Saturday â€” it depends on my family plans.
content.explanation: MIGHT expresses possibility/uncertainty â€” she hasn't decided and it's not certain.

content.question: Which sentence correctly uses MAY for possibility?
content.explanation: MAY + BASE VERB (no -ing, no 'to', no 's'). 'May rain' is correct.

content.question: Arjun says: 'I'm not 100% sure, but there's a good chance I'll get the promotion.' Which best expresses this?
options: I will definitely get the promotion.
I'm getting the promotion next week.
content.explanation: MAY = possible but not certain â€” exactly what 'not 100% sure but good chance' means.

content.question: What is the difference between 'I might come' and 'I'm coming'?
options: No difference â€” both mean the same thing.
'I might come' = possible, not sure. 'I'm coming' = fixed, arranged plan.
'I might come' = very certain. 'I'm coming' = uncertain.
'I might come' is past tense. 'I'm coming' is present tense.
content.correct: 'I might come' = possible, not sure. 'I'm coming' = fixed, arranged plan.
content.explanation: This is a key distinction: might = uncertain possibility; present continuous for future = confirmed arrangement.

content.instruction: Fill in with MAY, MIGHT, MAY NOT, or MIGHT NOT.

sentences.text: I ___ go to Ooty next month â€” it depends on whether I get leave approved.
She ___ be in the office today â€” she was feeling sick yesterday.
The results ___ be announced tomorrow or it ___ take until next week.
Don't wait for me for dinner â€” I ___ be late. The traffic is terrible.

content.instruction: Rewrite using MAY or MIGHT to make the sentence less certain.

sentences.text: It will rain this afternoon. â†’ It ___ this afternoon.
She will get the promotion. â†’ She ___ the promotion.
They won't finish on time. â†’ They ___ not finish on time.
He will move to Singapore. â†’ He ___ to Singapore.

content.answer: I might apply for that job â€” not sure yet.

content.instruction: Arrange into a negative possibility sentence.
content.answer: We may not make it to the flight in time.

content.instruction: Match the uncertain situation to the best MAY/MIGHT sentence.

pairs.left: Boss might give promotion â€” not decided
pairs.right: She may get a promotion this quarter.

pairs.left: Not sure if friend will join dinner
pairs.right: Meera might not come â€” she seemed busy.

pairs.left: Not certain the train will be on time
Considering a holiday but not booked
pairs.right: I might go to Kerala in December.

content.title: Planning a trip with uncertainty â€” Pune
content.setting: Kavya and her friend Ravi are trying to plan a trip to Himachal Pradesh but are unsure about many things.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Ravi, I've been thinking about that Himachal trip we discussed. Do you think it'll actually happen this year?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: It might! I may be able to take leave in October, but it depends on the project deadline.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: October sounds good â€” the weather might be perfect there. But what if it snows already?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: It may snow in higher areas like Spiti, but Manali and Kasol might still be okay in October.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Should we book early? Or might prices come down closer to the date?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: Flights might get cheaper, but hotels may fill up fast in October â€” it's peak season. I'd book hotels early.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: True. What about Sonal â€” might she join us?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: She may come. She said she might take a long break in October, but she may not be able to afford it right now.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: I see. And transport â€” should we rent a car?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: We might rent a car or we may just take cabs from Manali. Depends on the group size.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Okay, so nothing is confirmed yet?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: Not yet! But it might all come together by next month. Let's decide by August.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Alright. It might be the best trip we've ever taken â€” I can feel it!

comprehension_questions.question: Why might Ravi not be able to go in October?
options: It depends on his project deadline.
comprehension_questions.correct: It depends on his project deadline.

comprehension_questions.question: Why does Ravi say to book hotels early?
options: Hotels may fill up fast â€” it's peak season.
comprehension_questions.correct: Hotels may fill up fast â€” it's peak season.

comprehension_questions.question: Why might Sonal not join the trip?
options: She may not be able to afford it right now.
comprehension_questions.correct: She may not be able to afford it right now.

sentences.wrong: She might to come to the party.
sentences.correct: She might come to the party.

sentences.explanation: MIGHT never changes â€” no 's' for any person.

sentences.wrong: I will maybe go to the market later.
sentences.correct: I might go to the market later. / I may go to the market later.
sentences.explanation: 'Will maybe' is not standard. Use MAY or MIGHT for uncertainty.

content.scenario: Your colleague asks if you'll attend the optional team outing on Friday. You'd like to go but you have a family commitment that might finish in time. What do you say?
options: I will come to the outing.
I'm not coming to the outing.
I might come â€” it depends on whether I finish my family commitment in time.
I will maybe come if I want.
content.correct: I might come â€” it depends on whether I finish my family commitment in time.
content.explanation: MIGHT is correct for genuine uncertainty. 'Will maybe' is not natural English.

content.instruction: Talk about 5 things in your life that are POSSIBLE but not certain in the next few months. Use may, might, may not, and might not.
content.prompt: Share things that might happen for you soon â€” plans, changes, possibilities you're considering.
content.example_response: There are several things in my life right now that are possible but not confirmed. First, I might get a transfer to our Hyderabad office â€” my manager mentioned it but nothing is decided. I may apply for a part-time online course in data science â€” I've been thinking about it for months but I'm not sure about the time commitment. My family might move to a bigger flat â€” we've been looking but haven't found the right place yet. My friend and I may go on a road trip to Coorg in September, but it depends on whether we both get leave approved at the same time. And I might finally start that YouTube channel I've been planning â€” I may not though, because I'm still a bit nervous about putting myself out there.

content.instruction: A friend asks you 5 questions about next week. Answer each one using may or might â€” because you're genuinely not certain about any of them.
content.prompt: Answer these 5 questions with uncertain responses: 1. Are you free next Saturday? 2. Will you come to the team lunch? 3. Will it be a busy week for you? 4. Are you going to the gym next week? 5. Will you be in the office on Friday?
content.example_response: Great questions! Let me answer honestly. Am I free next Saturday? I might be free â€” it depends on whether my cousin's visit from Coimbatore happens or not. She may come this weekend or she may postpone. Will I come to the team lunch? I may come if I finish my morning deliverables on time. I might be in back-to-back calls until 2 PM though, so it depends. Will it be a busy week? It might be very busy actually â€” we have a client review on Wednesday which might run long. Am I going to the gym? I may go on Tuesday and Thursday, but I might skip if work gets too hectic. And will I be in the office on Friday? I might work from home on Friday â€” my manager may allow it. So the honest answer to all five is: it depends!

content.instruction: Complete the weather forecast using MAY and MIGHT.
content.text:
Tomorrow's weather forecast for South India:

Chennai: Skies ___ (1) remain cloudy through the morning. There ___ (2) be light showers in the afternoon, especially in coastal areas. Temperatures ___ (3) drop slightly â€” carry a light jacket if you're going out in the evening.

Bangalore: The morning ___ (4) be pleasant with some sunshine. However, by evening, there ___ (5) be heavy rain â€” thunderstorms are possible. Outdoor plans ___ (6) need to be reconsidered.

Coimbatore: It ___ (7) be a dry day, but there's a small chance of drizzle near the Nilgiris. Visibility ___ (8) be low in the ghat sections after 4 PM â€” drive carefully.

content.title: Lesson 5 complete â€” May & might
key_points: Both: MAY/MIGHT + BASE VERB (no 'to', no -ing, no 's')
Use when NOT SURE â€” not 'I will maybe'
May and might are often interchangeable in everyday English
content.next_lesson: Possibility in conversation â€” hedging and expressing doubt
